sentences.text: Well then, the ghetto was set up, which was just a little bit sort of at the edge of town, near the railway station.
And people were starting to be taken into the ghettos.
We were not-- at that stage, we were-- we were still under house arrest.
And I do believe that that was on account of my father.
But then, one day-- to be exact, it would have been about the 16th of May-- that my father had instructions to immediately leave.
Now, there is a slight difference in our recollection, with my sister, on-- on this point, but I'm going to talk about my recollection, because I feel that-- that's how I remember it.
So my father had to leave within 24 hours.
And I do-- I remember-- he-- he left on the 17th of May.
And I remember him standing there saying goodbye to my mother and saying, you know, don't worry.
We'll-- this won't last long.
It will be all right.
And you know, I-- I-- I can see him-- still see him standing there.
Anyway, he left.
Now-- Who came to collect him?
Do you remember?
No, nobody-- I can't remember another-- unless the persons were outside.
That's possible.
But I can't actually physically remember a person there.
But now that you're-- you're mentioning it, there probably was someone outside waiting for him.
But he had to literally leave straight away.
Now, the way I remember it is that he was posted to a small country hospital where he-- he was-- well, working, working as a-- as a-- as a doctor.
But my memory is that he was virtually hidden there, because they did want to save him.
And he could have, in fact, not been taken out of the country, had he not heard that we have been.
And he actually wanted to go to Auschwitz, because he knew that his family had been taken.
So this is-- this is how I remember it.
The question is my sister remembers it slightly differently in that she says that my father was in charge of a-- a Jewish-- Jewish brigade-- in charge of-- I beg your pardon.
I don't mean in charge of-- as a doctor, that he was the doctor of a Jewish brigade.
I didn't know that.
So be that as it may, he didn't-- certainly didn't have to leave the country.
He left because he literally volunteered.
Anyway, so my father was gone.
So now, we were left in the-- in the house-- in-- in the flat for another very short time, maybe two or three weeks.
And then, we were-- we-- we had instructions to pack and into the ghetto.
They actually came for us.
I remember being walked through--
They-- I guess the best way I can-- well, I can call them Hungarian gendarmes.
They weren't soldiers, and they weren't the police.
It was in between.
Terrible people, probably the worst that you could ever imagine, as bad as all the Germans, the Gestapo, you name it.
Worse than the Army, worse than the police, they were just unbelievable.
Trained-- they were truly trained animals.
Anyway, they took us in.
Now, the ghetto-- it was-- it was rather a coincidence.
I had a very good friend, whom I-- whom I went to school with.
And she lived in that area.
She wasn't Jewish.
And she lived in that area.
And of all things, we were put into their flat.
Of course, we weren't the only ones in the flat, because every room had, ah, God knows how many people.
But at least I was somewhere where I had been before.
And I had some memories, nice memories, with that flat.
So the ghetto itself wasn't quite as horrible as one would-- one would imagine.
Obviously, we were hungry, naturally.
And-- and we were utterly restricted in the sense that you couldn't go outside of the ghetto walls.
They were wood.
They-- they put these wooden-- these wooden fences up.
And-- and they were quite high, although we did climb up to have look.
[LAUGHS] But they were quite high.
And-- but within there, we could-- we could move.
Now, you could go out and walk the streets, and-- and-- and naturally, we made friends immediately with other young people.
And so while it was dreadful, it wasn't-- I don't remember it as the most horrendous thing.
Maybe because what came after was so horrendous that the ghetto sort of went into, you know, in-- in-- into the background.
I do remember one thing, though.
We started hearing stories there.
The people were starting to say that we are-- none of us will survive.
And, of course, my sister and I kept saying, this is nonsense.
This is utter nonsense.
How can they think of such a thing?
And-- and-- and we truly believed that nothing can happen to us.
And this-- this is just nonsense.
Nothing can happen to us.
How can they even think that people could be killed?
Anyway, we started hearing that, very soon, we'll be taken out of the country.
And again, my sister and I were saying, this is nonsense, you know?
We'll never be taken out.
No.
Anyhow, sure enough, one morning-- as I remember it-- we had no-- no warning.
It was just banging on the doors and saying, in X number of hours-- I don't know, three hours, whatever-- you've got to be such and such and out.
And this-- How long-- how long had you been in the ghetto?
It couldn't have been more than about four weeks.
So we didn't really have a lot of time, because my father left on the 17th of May.
We probably had another two weeks, I should think, at home.
So-- and we left on the third of-- fourth-- fourth of July, we left Hungary.
So it couldn't have been too much time.
I-- I would think about four weeks.
So we were lined up, and we were marched out of the ghetto by-- on foot.
And we were marched quite some distance.
And this was, of course, July, early July, and very, very hot.
And we were marched right out of town.
And we were taken-- the first stop was a-- I can't think of the word-- where animals live.
Barn?
A barn.
That's right.
Thank you.
And it was a-- a-- huge barn.
And a whole lot of us-- I can't-- I wouldn't like to say how many, but a whole lot-- lot of us were-- were put in there.
And it didn't have any windows, it only had slits for-- I guess, for the animals.
And I remember seeing guns poked into those holes.
So they-- the guns were at ready.
Same.
The gendarmes.
And they locked the door, of course.
You couldn't go in and out, moving around.
And the-- a lot of people started crying and even wailing.
So there was quite a lot of noise.
And these-- these gendarmes started screaming at us, if you didn't shut up-- oh naturally, dirty, filthy Jews, you name it-- if you don't shut up, we'll-- we'll burn the barn down, with you in it.
And then, it was, if you don't shut up, we'll start shooting.
And we knew that they mean it.
We knew that.
We started to understand that we are not people anymore, that we're entirely in the hands of these monsters.
And we didn't get anything to eat.
I think, if I remember correctly, I certainly can't remember even getting any water at that stage.
It was absolutely awful.
And we were sitting on the floor, of course.
There was nothing else.
And we were just clinging to each other and-- and waiting to wake up, because it was just so horrendous.
From-- even from the ghetto, which was still-- there was a bed, there was a table-- we were left alone.
We hardly saw any-- well, they were around, naturally.
But we-- they didn't come up to you.
There-- you know, there was nothing-- we had nothing to do with them.
And then, suddenly, from one second to another, we ceased to be human, literally ceased to be human.
So anyway, the night went.
We didn't sleep, of course, but night went.
And next morning we were got up, and we went for another-- we-- we were taken further out.
And then-- then we were in-- we-- we came to some huge barracks, unused barracks, but we weren't taken inside.
We were in the courtyard outside.
And there was a huge machine gun facing us, right in front of us.
And the same story, you know?
If you make any noise at all-- and-- and they meant it.
There was someone always there at the machine gun.
And everybody had guns at the ready, because there were a lot of people-- Jews, a lot of Jews.
And I guess that they didn't know what the reactions were going to be, so everyone was heavily armed.
And-- and that's really the first time that we started seeing people bashed, although not the way we eventually saw people bashed.

But still, that was the first place where we realized that people can be hit, you know, that they-- that there is such a thing as just walking past a person and lashing out with either the-- the gun-- you know, the end of the-- barrel of the gun-- or hell, whatever.
No.
No.
No, they didn't.
You see, this is a very interesting phenomenon, really, but you truly couldn't.
First of all, the great majority were women, because the men, the younger men, were all taken out of-- of the country.
The were in-- in Russia.
They were in-- in forced-- they weren't soldiers, but anyway, they were taken out from Hungary.
And so there were old people, sure.
Old men.
And there were a whole lot of women and youngsters.
Now who-- and none of us knew what a gun was.
[LAUGHS] You-- you-- you weren't ready.
You weren't-- there was no organization.
There was nothing.
And the Hungarians was so anti-Jewish that you-- you had nowhere to run.
Not in the country.
In Budapest, it was different, because Budapest was big.
There was a million people there.
And nobody-- well, I say nobody knew anybody.
You know, you could disappear.
You can't disappear in the country.
Where?
How?
And no one would give you any help.
So no, nobody.
Nobody tried to escape.
Anyway, we were then, on-- on this second day, after being taken out of the ghetto, we were put into-- into the-- the wagons.
No, there weren't, because this was right out of the-- of the-- the town.
It was-- well, I wouldn't like to say how far, but pretty far out.
It was very isolated.
So no, there were no bystanders.
And-- and they were very careful that-- you see, even with the ghetto, when we climbed up to have a look, if anyone stopped around the ghetto walls outside, they were-- they were sent on.
You-- they weren't allowed-- no one was allowed to-- to gather, because, I guess, they didn't know if any-- if there's going to be any resistance, or you know, whatever.
So they were very careful of that.
So I can't remember any bystanders there at all.
I do in Germany, but not there, then, in Hungary.
So we were put into the wagons, the cattle-- cattle train.
And you know, to try and describe that journey is, honestly, virtually impossible.
There were, I would say, 70 to 80 people to-- to a-- a-- a cattle truck.
Some couldn't sit down, others could.
There were two buckets at either end.
It is true that the buckets where every so often emptied.
I don't know how often, but I know that I do remember the bucket being emptied.
I thought back very carefully about, you know, of those days, and I do now remember that it was.
But it might as well not have, because what's the use of putting a bucket out after 12 hours of use?
[SCOFFS] 70 people to two buckets.
So you can imagine the stink.
It was unbelievable.
The heat was unbelievable.
And there was a-- there was a window, a small window, but it wasn't open.
It had wire across it.
So it was, you know, wired up, heavily wired up.
So you got a little bit of lighting and a little bit of airing, but only just enough not to suffocate.
So can you imagine 70 people-- and naturally, we had nothing to eat, not a thing to eat, right through the three days.
That's July the fourth that we were put into-- into the wagons, into the cattle train.
And we arrived on the seventh.
And in that time, we had no food.
Now, there were a number of old people there, naturally, quite old people.
And-- and there were-- there were, at least I would say, three deaths in-- in the-- in the train, because the heat was just so incredible, you know?
People just couldn't-- couldn't stand up to it.
And the wailing was unbelievable.
And my-- my young sister was, of course, next to my mother.
And I-- I-- I remember her saying to my mother, you won't let them separate me from you, will you?
And, of course, my mother kept saying, never.
No, never.
Never.
She must have somehow felt something.
And we-- my-- my other sister-- well, actually, my little sister as well-- we actually stood up to the strain, comparatively speaking, pretty well.
But we were terrified, absolutely terrified.
We weren't getting sick.
We didn't faint.
There was no-- there wasn't anything that I can say to you, this and this happened.
It didn't.

But the psychological fact of being in a cattle truck with 70 people in that unbelievable heat, not knowing where we're being taken-- and every time the train stopped-- which was often, because they'd shunt-- they shunted somewhere, and another train would go by-- we would see these-- these gendarmes outside.
It was still not Germans at this stage.
It was still gendarmes outside, with the-- with the guns.
And we could hear what they were saying.
And they-- they were using the most awful words about the Jews in there.
So we knew that whatever was happening would-- wasn't happening to people, it was happening to animals.
We were-- but cattle wouldn't have been shut in like that.
No, cattle would-- would have had better treatment, much better treatment than we did.
It was just unbelievable.
And I remember, as-- as one could see out every so often-- well, you could see out between these-- these wires.
And I remember, somewhere, we saw a-- a building.
There was-- we were going through-- must have been going through a town somewhere.
And-- and I saw-- I saw buildings not very far from the train line.
And I saw someone, a woman, lean out the window with-- with-- I don't know what was in her hand.
I think it was-- I think it was a-- a small rug.
And she was obviously cleaning the rug.
And she had a-- head-- you know, one of those caps on, to keep the dust off of her hair.
And the incongruity of this-- you know, I'll never forget this.
It was-- it was like an obscene joke.
And I thought, this woman-- all of this must have gone through my head so quickly, that this woman is cleaning her flat.
She's living like a human being.
She's probably going to cook for her husband for lunch in a number of, you know, in hours.
What I'm trying to say is that the realization that there are still people out there living like human beings.
And here we are in this cattle truck being-- in-- in this unbelievable, dreadful situation-- taken somewhere where we don't know where.
It was enough to send you crazy.
It really was.
You know, I don't know whether-- it-- it-- and-- and I don't know if anyone-- if everyone gets claustrophobic.
I do.
And that feeling of claustrophobia, of being shut in, in the hands-- even though you weren't, of course, literally-- but in the hands of these animals-- I wish I could tell you.
I can't.
It is not possible.
You know, the panic, the-- the fear, the unbelievable terror that was gripping one.
And it wasn't that it was for two minutes, and then it'd be all right.
It was just growing and growing.
Anyway, when we arrived in Auschwitz-- at least when we arrived, we didn't know where-- by then, people were fainting, and people were-- it was-- by then, it was fairly quiet, because it was spent.
You know, people just-- they were spent.
We were so hungry, as you can imagine.
We were so thirsty.
And that filth state, it was just unbelievable.
So we arrived somewhere.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as the train drew into this somewhere, and we were trying to see, naturally, someone saw the word Auschwitz.
And then, an incredible scream went up.
So obviously, a lot of people did know where we were.
We didn't, but they obviously knew, because it was-- it was the most awful wail that you could ever hear, you know, this-- this-- this-- this-- again, terror, absolute terror.
So we were all trying to-- to look out.
And of course, my sister and I, we were, being so young, we did stand up to the whole thing far better than a whole of the others.
So we were up, trying to look out on this-- on-- on-- on this little window, or whatever.
And the first thing that I remember computing in my mind-- well first of all, we saw the fences, but we didn't know what the fences were about, but we saw these fences.
And on the fences-- it was a windy day.
And on the fences were something, bunches of things, here and there, that were waving in the wind, just stuck to the fence.
And we were saying, what on Earth could this be?
What could it be?
What is this?
And then I remember we were saying, it sort of looks like hair, but it can't be hair.
So what-- what is it?
What is it?
It was hair.
It was-- of course, the-- the-- the fence was electrified wire.
And it was the easiest way to suicide.
So people ran into the wire.
And with the incredibly high electricity, it literally scalped them.
And the hair stuck on the fence.
And the hair was just waving there.
And you know-- not-- not then.
I-- I didn't think of that then, but later on, because that-- that became a normal thing around you in the camp.
There was these-- these hairs, these scalps everywhere.
And then I thought sometimes to myself, my goodness, that is someone's daughter, someone's mother, someone's grandmother, someone-- it's a person.
It's a person.
That hair belonged to a person.
But that wasn't so bad.
What stuck us-- struck us was they didn't have any hair-- completely shaven off.

And the eyes were a sort of-- [MAKES EYES BIG] as if they didn't know where they were, just, you now, the vague looking around of people who really don't know who they are, or where they are, or what--
So we decided that these must be insane.
So this obviously is an area where the insane is being kept, because quite obviously, they-- they obviously are mad.
[INAUDIBLE] because all these things-- in-- in-- in-- through the wires, so you couldn't truly tell what you saw.
You just had impressions of things.
But by then-- of course, the train was very slow-- because we were inside.
So we got in.
The train stopped.
And as I remember, the ramp, rather like a-- a U, but not rounded U, but that way, that way, that way.
So we came in on this side.
And the doors eventually opened.
And we immediately, of course, which was what we had right through everywhere-- schnell, schnell, schnell, schnell, you know?
Quickly, out, out, out.
Jews, get out, you know?
And if people weren't quick enough, then, of course, the whips came out.
So people were literally whipped off the train.
And I remember one woman, who were-- who was quite obviously petrified, and-- and got, sort of, psychologically stuck inside of the opening of the-- the cattle truck.
And she was whipped, and she fell out.
Well, again, she screamed first.
And then, she fell out.
Again, I suppose in vain, I'll try and explain what this means when you're, for the first time, truly, in your life, faced with this sort of inhumanity.
It's-- this is over 50 years ago now, of course.
This was in 1944.
And to me, I can see the expression on her face.
It just burns into your memory, because the shock is so horrendous that it'll never leave you.
And there is things that, to this day, to me-- you know, I know perfectly well that this has caused this, and this, and this, and this, because the shock to one's system was such that it was forever.
It just burnt in.
And there it stays, never to be moved again.
Anyway, we were eventually-- not eventually, but fairly quickly then-- out of-- of cattle trucks.
We had to be-- we-- we to jump down.
And of course, these old people-- because there was some very old people that had to be-- no one was helped down, as you can imagine.
They just had to make the best of it.
It was just awful.
And of course, opposite, right opposite us, we could see all these armed SS, armed to the teeth.
Because now, we got the SS.
That's really when we first start-- and-- and every single one of them had, naturally, not-- not-- not rifles, pistols.
Many of them had these huge, vicious-looking dogs.
You could tell that these dogs will eat you up in no time at all.
They all had whips.
So it was like a scene out of an inferno.
So we were-- we got off.
And we were told to stand five in a line, which was always.
It was always five in a line.
So it was my mother and us three girls, so that's four of us.
And one other person, an older person from-- from our hometown happened to be.
So my mother had her-- her medicine bag with her.
She carried that.
That is the only thing that we had with us.
And she carried that.
And a German came by and-- and said, immediately, you know, what's that?
And my mother said, you know, it's my medicine bag.
And [INAUDIBLE] he said, you know, doctor?
Yes.
And said, come with me.
So he took my mother, immediately.
And that was it.
We didn't know where, how.
That was it.
My mother disappeared.
So there we were, my sisters, myself, and that one other woman.
And then, as were only four of us then, another person from my hometown stepped back into our line.
Now, she happened to be the wife of a-- a professor, who my mother worked with.
So at least, this was a-- because she was an older woman.
I don't know how old she would have been, but to me, she looked ancient.
She was probably all of 50, if that.
Yeah, it would have been about that, because my mother was 44.
Yeah.
But, you know, to me, she was ancient.
And-- but at least it was someone we knew, so that was good.
And so there was [?
Jocka, ?] my older sister, [?
Suzy, ?] my young sister, and then-- then myself.
And on both sides, were two other women.
Now, in the center, opposite, was a table.
And there sat an SS man, who was, of course, Mengele.
And people were walking up to him.
And whatever was being said, we didn't know.
And some-- there were ticks made-- and some people went that way, and the others went that way.
As it happened, in our case, the right-hand side was the gas chamber, and the left-hand side was the alive.
But of course, we didn't know that, need I say?
We had no idea about that.
Thank God.
Anyway, we were slowly going up towards.
And I really don't quite know how-- I think what may have happened-- you know, bush telegrams work immediately.
And somehow, the word came back that they're asking ages.
They're asking what your age is.
So my sister and I, for God knows what reason, decided that we are going to say that we're older.
We did look much older.
We were both-- I was as tall as I am now.
I-- I didn't-- didn't grow one-millionth of an inch since then.
And we both looked-- see, I was 13, [?
Jocka ?], 14, but we looked far older than that.
So we decided that I'm going to be 17, and [?
Jocka's ?] going to be 18.
God knows why we did that.
So when we got there, this very good-looking SS man-- he really was a good-looking man-- turned to my sister and-- and pointed to Zsuzsa, my younger sister, said, is that your daughter?
And my sister said, no, she's my sister.
And sent-- immediately sent my younger sister to the right.
And she then stopped there.
My younger sister just stopped there.
And he turned to us, and said, how old are you?
My sister said, 18.
How old am I? 17.
So he said, that way.
Well, the thing that, of course, terrified us at that moment was, what is going to-- where is our sister going to be taken?
And I don't know whom we asked.
I have no idea, but I know that we did talk to somebody, and said, you know, what's happening?
And whoever it was said, don't worry.
The young people and the old people go that way, because there will be a truck waiting for them.
They'll be taken by car, where you are going to walk.
So don't worry, you'll meet them up-- so we were quite-- we accepted that.
Now, in the meantime, the-- the professor's wife had also been-- had her interview with Mengele.
And she was sent the same way as my younger sister was.
So she took my young sister's hand, and they started walking away.
And my sister looked back and just sort of looked at us.
And we waved and said, we'll see you.
Don't worry.
We'll see you.
And we said, she'll take care of you.
We'll see you.
And that was that.
We never saw her again.
So we then-- by this, of course, hours had passed.
And we were taken to-- well, before I go any further, I would like to say something here.
That this-- I believe that we survived by a series of incredible miracles, which we did.
And by that, I don't mean God-given miracle.
Unfortunately, I cannot believe that.
But some sort of choice miracle.
Who knows?

My mother, had she not had her medical bag there, would have died with my younger sister, because in that transport-- I understand not in every one, but certainly from then on-- this was in '44-- every mother died with the young child, I guess because they were frightened of-- of trouble.
So she would have gone with my sister, young sister-- dead.
But she didn't, because she was picked out by a German.
My sister said Zsuzsa is not her daughter.
Had she said yes-- which she might have, just to be sure that they're together-- she would have been sent to the gas chamber.
Had I said I'm 13, I would have had a very fair chance to go straight to the gas chamber.
And we didn't.
My mother survived.
My sister survived.
I survived.
So that was, what I call, the first incredible choice that we made, which was just a choice.
Anyway--
Oh, right.
